docs.title: Welcome to National 5 Computing Science
docs.text:
National 5 Computing Science helps you develop knowledge and understanding of computing science. You will learn how computer systems work, how data is represented, and how digital solutions are designed, created, tested and evaluated.

The course also develops computational thinking and practical problem-solving skills. These skills are used to solve problems in software, databases and websites.

docs.title: Course Content
docs.text:
The course has four areas of study:

  • Software Design and Development
  • Computer Systems
  • Database Design and Development
  • Web Design and Development

However, the course assessment is not the same for every learner. Everyone studies the mandatory areas, then completes one optional area.

Important

Software Design and Development and Computer Systems are mandatory.

You will then study either Database Design and Development or Web Design and Development.

Your optional area is assessed in both the question paper and the practical assignment.

docs.title: Software Design and Development
docs.text:
In this area, you will develop programming and computational-thinking skills. You will learn how to analyse problems, design solutions, implement programs, test them and evaluate how well they work.

You will learn about:

  • Development methodologies
  • Analysis, design, implementation, testing and evaluation
  • Programming using a high-level language
  • Data types and structures
  • Selection and repetition
  • Standard algorithms
  • Testing with normal, extreme and exceptional data
  • Evaluating software solutions

docs.title: Computer Systems
docs.text:
In this area, you will learn how computer systems store and process data. You will also learn about basic computer architecture, environmental impact and security precautions.

You will learn about:

  • Binary representation of positive integers
  • Floating point representation
  • Extended ASCII
  • Bitmap and vector graphics
  • Basic computer architecture
  • Translators, including interpreters and compilers
  • Environmental impact of computer systems
  • Firewalls and encryption

docs.title: Optional Area
docs.text: You will study one of the following optional areas.

docs.title: Database Design and Development
docs.text:
In this area, you will develop knowledge, understanding and practical problem-solving skills in database design and development.

You will learn about:

  • Analysis of database problems
  • End-user and functional requirements
  • UK GDPR
  • Entity-relationship diagrams
  • Data dictionaries
  • Primary keys and foreign keys
  • Relational databases with two linked tables
  • SQL operations including SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE and DELETE
  • Testing and evaluating database solutions

docs.title: Web Design and Development
docs.text:
In this area, you will develop knowledge, understanding and practical problem-solving skills in web design and development.

You will learn about:

  • Analysis of website problems
  • End-user and functional requirements
  • Website structure
  • User-interface design and wireframes
  • Copyright law relating to web content
  • File formats and compression
  • HTML
  • CSS
  • JavaScript mouse events
  • Testing and evaluating web-based solutions

docs.title: Question Paper
docs.text:
The question paper is worth 80 marks and lasts 1 hour and 30 minutes.

The question paper has three sections. Section 1 is mandatory. You then complete either Section 2 or Section 3.

Question Paper Section Area Assessed Marks Section 1 Software Design and Development, and Computer Systems 55 Section 2 Database Design and Development 25 Section 3 Web Design and Development 25

Question paper choice

Everyone completes Section 1.

You then complete either Section 2 or Section 3, depending on whether you are studying Database Design and Development or Web Design and Development.

docs.title: Practical Assignment
docs.text:
The practical assignment is worth 40 marks and is completed under supervised open-book conditions.

The assignment has three tasks. Task 1 is mandatory. You then complete either Task 2 or Task 3.

Computer Systems is not assessed in the practical assignment.

Assignment Task Area Assessed Marks Task 1 Software Design and Development 25 Task 2 Database Design and Development 15 Task 3 Web Design and Development 15

Assignment choice

Everyone completes Task 1.

You then complete either Task 2 or Task 3, depending on whether you are studying Database Design and Development or Web Design and Development.

docs.title: Overall Course Assessment
docs.text:
Your final grade is based on the total marks achieved across the question paper and the practical assignment.

Assessment Component Marks Percentage Question Paper 80 67% Practical Assignment 40 33% Total 120 100%

docs.title: Skills Developed
docs.text:
By studying National 5 Computing Science, you will develop skills in:

  • Computational thinking
  • Problem solving
  • Analysing problems
  • Designing digital solutions
  • Implementing solutions
  • Testing and evaluation
  • Communicating computing concepts using correct terminology
  • Understanding the impact of computing technologies

docs.title: Progression
docs.text:
Successful completion of National 5 Computing Science can lead to:

  • Higher Computing Science
  • Other qualifications in Computing Science or related areas
  • Further study, employment or training

docs.title: What is a Software Development Methodology?
docs.text:
To answer this, we will split the question into two parts, Software and Development Methodology.

As you already know, all computer systems are made up of hardware and software.

The term hardware is easy to understand, because you can touch it. For example, the processor, RAM, network adaptor and graphic cards etc...

Software, however, is not so obvious………………

Software is all the programs, instructions and data that allow the hardware to do something useful and interesting. Some of the most used include; word, excel, PowerPoint & Google (other browsers are available!)

Tip

Software Development is defined as the process of designing, creating, testing, and maintaining computer programs and applications. There are many different Software Development Methodologies that all follow a different path through the Development Process.

docs.title: The Waterfall Method
docs.text:
The development methodology you will be learning is known as a Waterfall model which consists of SIX different steps.

A benefit of the Waterfall Methodology is that we can go back a step if there is a problem and fix it before then repeating the steps in order until we have the full working solution.

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text:
What is the purpose of the program?

What features will it have? (This is known as functional requirements)

What are the inputs, process and outputs?

What data structures and data types will be used?

docs.title: Design
docs.text:
The more planning you do the quicker the creation of the final product will be

Break a problem into chunks

Use diagrams to create and algorithm

Pseudocode, Structure Diagrams and Flowcharts

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
Create the solution to the problem

Use previously designed Algorithms to signpost the way

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
All work needs to be checked thoroughly for errors.

Rigorous testing to systematically check every single operation/event.

Rigorously test a range of scenarios to make sure the program does what it is supposed to.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
Collate diagrams, algorithms and testing tables.

These documents together form the wider documentation (user guide & technical guide).

It is important that documentation is clear.

Documentation should also show what each person did and what has happened thus far.

docs.title: Evaluation
docs.text:
Fit for Purpose - does it do what it is supposed to do?

Efficient use of Coding Constructs - correct use of repetition, arrays (instead of variables) and selection statements where possible to reduce the amount of code?

Robustness – can it cope with unexpected or incorrect input?

Readability - it is important to write code that can be read and understood by others. This should include internal commentary, meaningful identifiers, indentation and white space.

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text:
What you Need to Know

Identify the purpose and functional requirements of a problem that relates to the design and implementation of a program.

Functional requirements are identified in terms of:

  • Inputs
  • Processes
  • Outputs

docs.title: What is Analysis?
docs.text:
Analysis is the first stage of software development.

During analysis, the programmer works out:

  • what the program needs to do
  • what information is needed
  • what processing must take place
  • what results the program should produce

One of the most important tasks in analysis is identifying the functional requirements.

docs.title: Functional Requirements
docs.text:
Functional requirements describe the tasks a program must perform.

At National 5, functional requirements are usually identified as:

Requirement Type Description Example Input Data entered into or collected by the program Username, age, score Process The work carried out by the program Calculating an average, sorting data Output Information produced by the program Messages, reports, results

A useful way to remember this is:

Input → Process → Output

Data goes in, the computer does something with it, and information comes out.

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text:
Inputs are the data that enter the program.

Common National 5 examples include:

  • keyboard input
  • mouse clicks
  • touchscreen presses
  • sensor readings
  • data read from a file

docs.title: Examples of Inputs
docs.text: Program Input Quiz program User's answer School registration system Student ID Weather station Temperature reading Online shop Product selected

docs.title: Processes
docs.text:
Processes are the actions carried out by the program.

The process is often described as the program's thinking.

Examples include:

  • calculations
  • comparisons
  • searching
  • sorting
  • validation checks
  • updating totals

docs.title: Examples of Processes
docs.text: Program Process Quiz program Check if answer is correct Banking app Calculate account balance Online shop Calculate total cost School system Search for a pupil record

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Outputs are the results produced by the program.

Common outputs include:

  • text displayed on screen
  • images or graphics
  • sounds
  • printed documents
  • saved files

docs.title: Examples of Outputs
docs.text: Program Output Quiz program Final score Banking app Current balance GPS system Route displayed on a map Online shop Order confirmation

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: A program asks every pupil in a class for their age. The program then calculates the average age and displays the result.

docs.title: Step 1: Identify the Inputs
docs.text: • Ages entered by the pupils

docs.title: Step 2: Identify the Process
docs.text: • Calculate the average age

docs.title: Step 3: Identify the Output
docs.text:
• Average age displayed on the screen
Inputs Processes Outputs Ages Calculate average age Average age

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
A GPS navigation system asks the user for a destination. The system determines the current location, calculates a route and displays directions on a map.

Inputs Processes Outputs Destination entered by the user Calculate the best route Route shown on map Current location from GPS satellite Calculate journey time Estimated arrival time

docs.title: National 5 Exam Tip
docs.text:
When an exam question asks for inputs, processes and outputs:

  1. Look for the data entering the system.
  2. Look for the work being carried out.
  3. Look for the information being produced.

A good answer normally uses words taken directly from the scenario.

docs.title: Quick Check
docs.text: For each scenario, identify one input, one process and one output.

docs.title: Scenario 1
docs.text:
A library system allows a user to enter a book title and searches for the book.

Input Process Output ? ? ?

docs.title: Scenario 2
docs.text:
A game asks the player for their name, calculates a score and displays the result.

Input Process Output ? ? ?

docs.title: Answers
docs.text:
Scenario 1

  • Input: Book title
  • Process: Search for the book
  • Output: Search result

Scenario 2

  • Input: Player name
  • Process: Calculate score
  • Output: Score displayed

Identify all the inputs, processes and outputs for the program above using the I-P-O table below.

docs.text:
A smart phone app is needed to calculate the cost of electricity. The following information will be entered by the user:

  • Previous meter reading

docs.title: Design
docs.text:
Design is the planning stage of software development. Before a programmer writes any code, they should think carefully about how the solution will work, what data will be needed and how the user will interact with the program.

What you Need to Know

You must be able to:

Identify the data types and structures required for a problem that relates to the implementation at this level, as listed below.

Describe, identify, and be able to read and understand:
  • structure diagrams
  • flowcharts
  • pseudocode

Exemplify and implement one of the above design techniques to design efficient solutions to a problem.

Describe, and implement user-interface design, in terms of input and output, using a wireframe.

docs.title: What is Design?
docs.text:
Design is the process of creating a plan for a solution before implementation begins. Just as an architect creates plans before constructing a building, programmers create designs before writing code.

Design techniques help programmers organise their ideas, communicate solutions clearly and ensure that all requirements have been considered before development starts.

Efficient problem-solving in computing begins with careful planning and the use of appropriate design techniques to ensure solutions are both effective and easy to implement.

Before a programmer begins writing code, they should create a design for their solution. A good design acts as a blueprint, helping to identify potential problems early, organise ideas clearly and reduce mistakes during implementation. Spending time on design often saves time later in the development process.

At National 5 level, there are four key design techniques you need to understand:

  • Structure diagrams provide a visual breakdown of a solution, showing how a large problem can be divided into smaller, more manageable tasks.

  • Flowcharts illustrate the logical flow of processes, making it easier to follow the sequence of steps, decisions and actions within an algorithm.

  • Pseudocode bridges the gap between natural language and programming by offering a simplified, language-independent way to represent a solution before it is coded.

  • Wireframes are simple sketches or digital drafts that show the layout of a user interface. They help designers plan where input controls, buttons, menus, images and output areas will be placed before development begins.

Exam Tip

In the National 5 course, you may be asked to identify, describe, read or create design techniques. Make sure you understand both what each design technique looks like and when it should be used.

docs.title: Why is Design Important?
docs.text:
Good design helps programmers:

  • plan solutions before writing code
  • identify errors and missing requirements early
  • communicate ideas with other developers and stakeholders
  • create programs that are easier to test, maintain and improve

Throughout this section, you will learn how each design technique can be used to represent solutions and prepare for implementation.

docs.title: What Will You Learn Next?
docs.text:
The following pages explore each design technique in more detail:

  • Structure Diagrams – breaking problems into smaller tasks.
  • Flowcharts – representing algorithms visually.
  • Pseudocode – representing algorithms using structured English.
  • Wireframes – planning the layout of a user interface.

By the end of this section, you should be able to read, understand and create each of these design techniques for simple National 5 problems.

docs.title: Data Types and Data Structures
docs.text:
Data types and data structures are used during the design stage to help programmers decide how information will be stored in a program. Choosing the correct data type or data structure makes programs easier to create, test and maintain.

What you Need to Know

You must be able to identify the data types and structures required for a problem that relates to the implementation at this level.

docs.title: Data Structures
docs.text:
At National 5, the two data structures you need to know are:

  • Variable – stores a single value (e.g. age = 15)
  • Array – stores multiple values of the same type (e.g. scores = [14, 18, 20])

You must be able to identify both as data structures in the exam.

If you need to store... Use... One value Variable Many similar values Array

Exam Tip

National 5 questions often ask you to identify an appropriate data type or data structure for a piece of information.

Ask yourself:

  • Is it one value or many values?
  • Is it text, a whole number, a decimal number or True/False?

Variables

A variable is used to store a single value during the running of a program.

Although a variable only holds one value, it is still considered a data structure because it stores data in a named place in memory.

Variables
name1 = ("Alice")
name2 = ("Ben")
name3 = ("Carla")
name4 = ("David")
name5 = ("Eilidh")

Even though it holds only one value, the SQA considers a variable to be a data structure, because it is a named place in memory where data is stored.

Arrays

At National 5, the 2nd most common structure you will use is an array (also called a list).

An array lets you store many values in one place. For example, if you wanted to store the names of five pupils, instead of creating five separate variables, you could use one array:

Arrays
names = ["Alice", "Ben", "Carla", "David", "Eilidh"]

docs.title: Data Types
docs.text:
Data types are important when programming. The computer has to know whether to expect numbers, text, or other types of values.

There are five data types you must know for National 5:

Data Type Used For Integer Whole numbers (positive or negative), e.g. 7, -12 Real Numbers with decimal places, e.g. 3.14, -0.5 Boolean True or False values, often used in decisions or conditions Character A single letter, number, or symbol, e.g. 'A', '3', '?' String Text made up of multiple characters, e.g. "Hello123!"

If you are given an example, you need to be able to say which data type it belongs to.

Example Value Data Type "Ella" String 15 Integer 15.5 Real True Boolean

Notice that there is no example of a character in the table above.

A character stores a single letter, number or symbol:

initial = 'D'

A string stores multiple characters together:

name = "Dave"

Example

You have been asked to create a program that asks the user for the names and ages of everyone in the class.

When finished, it displays a message saying how many people are in the class, and their average age.

If you were asked to explain the data types used in this program, in the exam, you would say something like:

  • The names are strings, because they are text

  • The ages are integers, e.g. someone is 15 years old

  • The number of people in the class is an integer, because you can’t have 0.5 of a person

  • The average is a real number because it might include a decimal point.

The last point is really important - quite often, you could be asked to calculate an average.

Think about it yourself - if three people are 15, 15 and 16, those are integers - but the average is 15.333, a real number.

Remember to fully think through the problems!

Common Mistakes

  • Thinking an average will always be an integer.
  • Confusing characters and strings.
  • Using multiple variables when an array would be more appropriate.
  • Forgetting that arrays are used to store multiple values.

These data types are really important — you’ll use and discuss them in every program, and being confident with them will really help you in the exam.

docs.title: Before You Start
docs.text:
These tasks will help you practise choosing suitable data types and data structures.

Remember

  • String - text made from more than one character
  • Integer - a whole number
  • Real - a number with a decimal point
  • Boolean - True or False
  • Character - one letter, number or symbol
  • Variable - stores one value
  • Array - stores multiple values

For each question, write your answers in your Google Workbook.

Explain each answer using the data in the question.

docs.title: Task 1: Identify The Data Type
docs.text:
Question 1

State the most suitable data type for each value.

  • Hello World
  • 22.115
  • False
  • A
  • 66

Question 2

A program on a bank computer stores customer names and account balances.

Mrs S. Johnson has £122.01 in her account.

State the two data types being used.

For each one, say which value it is used for.

Info

Currency is not a data type in software development.

docs.title: Task 2: Identify The Data Structure
docs.text:
Question 1

A game asks for a user's nickname and score.

Identify:

  • the most suitable data structure
  • the data type for the player nickname
  • the data type for the player score

Explain each choice.

Question 2

A school register program stores the names of pupils and whether each pupil is present.

Once the teacher has entered the data, the program tells them how many pupils are present or absent.

Identify:

  • the most suitable data structure
  • the data type used for pupil names
  • the data type used to store whether each pupil is present
  • the data type used for the number of pupils present or absent

Explain each choice.

docs.title: Task 3: Choose Data Types For A Program
docs.text:
For these questions, identify the data a program needs to store or process. For each data item, ask yourself:

  • Is it text?
  • Is it a whole number?
  • Is it a number with a decimal point?
  • Is it True or False?
  • Is it a single character?

Choose from: string, integer, real, Boolean or character.

Example

A program stores the number of tickets a customer books.

Data item Example value Data type number of tickets 3 integer

Question 1

A supermarket self-scan app reads a product code, for example 128761, and finds the product name.

The customer then presses a button to confirm if that is the correct product.

The app adds the price of the product to their total amount due.

Identify four data items this program needs to store or process.

For each data item, state the most suitable data type.

Hint

Think about the product code, product name, button choice and money values.

Question 2

An online game asks users to log in with a username and password.

It counts the total number of hours they have spent playing the game.

It stores the player's high scores.

Identify:

  • four data items this program needs to store or process
  • the most suitable data type for each data item
  • one suitable data structure used by this program

Explain each choice.

Question 3

A cinema booking app lets a customer choose a film, screening time and number of tickets.

After the customer makes their choices, the app stores:

  • the film title
  • the screening time
  • the number of tickets booked
  • whether the booking has been confirmed
  • the total cost

Identify the five data items stored by the app.

For each data item, state the most suitable data type.

Hint

The data type describes the value being stored or processed.

For example, the number of tickets is an integer, because it is a whole number.

docs.title: Design Notation
docs.text:
Design notation is a way of showing the design of a program before it is coded. It helps programmers plan the steps, decisions and loops needed to solve a problem.

What you Need to Know

You must be able to:

Describe, identify, and be able to read and understand:

  • structure diagrams

  • flowcharts

  • pseudocode

Exemplify and implement one of the above design notations to design efficient solutions to a problem.

docs.title: What is Design Notation?
docs.text:
Design notation is the method used to represent the design of a program. It lets a programmer show how a solution will work without writing the final code.

At National 5 level, design notation is used to show the three programming constructs:

  • Sequencing - instructions carried out in order
  • Selection - a decision or choice
  • Iteration - repetition or looping

There are three main types of design notation used in this section:

  • Structure diagrams provide a visual breakdown of a solution, showing how a large problem can be divided into smaller, more manageable tasks.

  • Flowcharts illustrate the logical flow of processes, making it easier to follow the sequence of steps, decisions and actions within an algorithm.

  • Pseudocode bridges the gap between natural language and programming by offering a simplified, language-independent way to represent a solution before it is coded.

Exam Tip

In the National 5 course, you may be asked to identify, describe, read or create design notation. Make sure you understand what each notation looks like and when it should be used.

docs.title: Why is Design Notation Important?
docs.text:
Good design notation helps programmers:

  • plan solutions before writing code
  • identify errors and missing requirements early
  • communicate ideas with other developers and stakeholders
  • create programs that are easier to test, maintain and improve

Throughout this section, you will learn how sequencing, selection and iteration can be represented using structure diagrams, flowcharts and pseudocode.

Summary

  • Design notation is used to plan a program before it is coded.
  • Structure diagrams, flowcharts and pseudocode are types of design notation.
  • Design notation can show sequencing, selection and iteration.
  • Good design notation makes a solution easier to understand, test and implement.

docs.title: Sequencing
docs.text:
What you Need to Know

You must be able to describe, identify, understand and read:

  • sequencing
  • sequencing in structure diagrams
  • sequencing in flowcharts
  • sequencing in pseudocode

Sequencing is one of the three programming constructs that can be shown using design notation:

  • sequencing
  • selection
  • iteration

Sequencing means that instructions are carried out in a set order, one after another.

Most programs use sequencing. For example, a program that calculates the total cost of an order might:

  1. ask the user for the item price
  2. ask the user for the quantity
  3. calculate the total cost
  4. display the total cost

The order matters. The program cannot calculate the total cost until it has the item price and quantity.

docs.title: Structure Diagrams
docs.text:
In a structure diagram, sequencing is shown by placing sub-tasks from left to right in the order they should happen.

The top box shows the main task. The boxes underneath show the sub-tasks needed to complete it.

Structure diagrams use different symbols to show processes, selections, predefined processes and loops.

Structure diagram symbols

For the total-cost problem, the main task could be Calculate Total Cost. This can be broken down into four sub-tasks:

  1. Enter item price
  2. Enter quantity
  3. Calculate total cost
  4. Display total cost

These sub-tasks would be read from left to right. This shows the sequence of tasks.

Sequencing shown as a structure diagram

docs.title: Flowcharts
docs.text:
In a flowchart, sequencing is shown by arrows moving from one step to the next.

Flowcharts are usually read from top to bottom. If the flowchart is only showing sequencing, the arrows move from one step to the next without branching or looping.

Flowcharts use different symbols to show the start and end of a program, inputs and outputs, decisions and processes.

Flowchart symbols

For example:

Sequencing shown as a flowchart

Each step happens once and in order. There is no decision and no loop.

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
In pseudocode, sequencing is shown by writing instructions in the order they should happen.

Pseudocode is usually read from top to bottom.

For example:

Sequencing shown as pseudocode

Main Steps

1 Enter item price
2 Enter quantity
3 Calculate total cost
4 Display total cost

Refinements
3.1 totalCost = itemPrice * quantity
4.1 display totalCost

Each step is completed once, in order. There is no decision and no repetition.

Spotting Sequencing

To identify sequencing in design notation, look for instructions or tasks that:

  • happen one after another
  • are read in a clear order
  • happen once
  • do not branch in different directions
  • do not loop back to an earlier step

If the design includes a question, condition or different possible paths, it is showing selection. If it repeats a task, it is showing iteration.

Summary

  • Sequencing means carrying out instructions in order.
  • In a structure diagram, sequencing is shown from left to right.
  • In a flowchart, sequencing is shown by arrows moving from one step to the next.
  • In pseudocode, sequencing is shown by writing instructions in order.
  • A sequence does not include a decision or a loop.

docs.title: Sequencing Tasks
docs.text:
These tasks will help you practise showing sequencing using different types of design notation.

Use the same problem in each section, but represent it using a different notation.

Draw.io

Use draw.io to create your structure diagrams and flowcharts.

Export each diagram as an image and add it to your workbook.

docs.title: Structure Diagrams
docs.text:
For each task, create a structure diagram.

Remember:

  • the main task goes at the top
  • sub-tasks go underneath
  • sequencing is read from left to right
  • each task should happen once, in order

docs.title: Task 1: Total Wage
docs.text:
Create a structure diagram for a program that:

  1. asks the user for their hourly pay rate
  2. asks the user how many hours they have worked
  3. multiplies the hours worked by the hourly rate to calculate the total wage
  4. displays the total wage

Add an image of your structure diagram to your workbook.

docs.title: Task 2: Area of a Circle
docs.text:
Create a structure diagram for a program that:

  1. asks the user for the radius of a circle
  2. calculates the area using 2 * π * radius^2
  3. displays the area

Add an image of your structure diagram to your workbook.

docs.title: Task 3: Volume
docs.text:
Create a structure diagram for a program that:

  1. asks the user for the length
  2. asks the user for the breadth
  3. asks the user for the height
  4. calculates the volume
  5. displays the volume

Add an image of your structure diagram to your workbook.

docs.title: Task 4: Running Total
docs.text:
Create a structure diagram for a program that:

  1. asks the user to enter a number
  2. adds the number to a total
  3. repeats this until five numbers have been entered
  4. displays the total

Add an image of your structure diagram to your workbook.

Note

This task includes repetition, so it is a challenge question. Focus on showing the tasks in the correct order.

docs.title: Task 5: Test Scores
docs.text:
Create a structure diagram for a program that:

  1. asks the user to enter three unit test scores
  2. adds each score to a total
  3. calculates the average score
  4. displays the total and average

Add an image of your structure diagram to your workbook.

Note

This task includes repeated input, so it is a challenge question. Focus on showing the tasks in the correct order.

docs.title: Flowcharts
docs.text:
For each task, create a flowchart.

Remember:

  • start with a start symbol
  • use input/output symbols for entering and displaying data
  • use process symbols for calculations
  • sequencing is shown by arrows moving from one step to the next

docs.title: Task 1: Total Wage
docs.text:
Create a flowchart for a program that:

  1. asks the user for their hourly pay rate
  2. asks the user how many hours they have worked
  3. multiplies the hours worked by the hourly rate to calculate the total wage
  4. displays the total wage

Add an image of your flowchart to your workbook.

docs.title: Task 2: Area of a Circle
docs.text:
Create a flowchart for a program that:

  1. asks the user for the radius of a circle
  2. calculates the area using 2 * π * radius^2
  3. displays the area

Add an image of your flowchart to your workbook.

docs.title: Task 3: Volume
docs.text:
Create a flowchart for a program that:

  1. asks the user for the length
  2. asks the user for the breadth
  3. asks the user for the height
  4. calculates the volume
  5. displays the volume

Add an image of your flowchart to your workbook.

docs.title: Task 4: Running Total
docs.text:
Create a flowchart for a program that:

  1. asks the user to enter a number
  2. adds the number to a total
  3. repeats this until five numbers have been entered
  4. displays the total

Add an image of your flowchart to your workbook.

Note

This task includes repetition, so it is a challenge question.

docs.title: Task 5: Test Scores
docs.text:
Create a flowchart for a program that:

  1. asks the user to enter three unit test scores
  2. adds each score to a total
  3. calculates the average score
  4. displays the total and average

Add an image of your flowchart to your workbook.

Note

This task includes repeated input, so it is a challenge question.

For each task, write design pseudocode.

Use this format:

Main Steps

1 ...
2 ...

Refinements

2.1 ...
3.1 ...

docs.text:
