docs.title: Analysis
docs.text:
What you need to know

You must be able to identify the end-user and functional requirements of a database problem that relates to the implementation at this level.

When designing a database, it's important to understand what the people using the system need it to do. This means identifying:

  • End-user requirements – the tasks users need to complete
  • Functional requirements – the data, tables, keys, relationships and actions the database needs so those tasks can happen

docs.title: End-user Requirements and Functional Requirements
docs.text:
Before creating a database, we must find out what the people who will use it need to do. These are called the end-user requirements:

  • the users are the people who will use the database
  • their requirements are the tasks they expect to complete using the database
  • these are usually written from the user's point of view

We also need to identify what the database must contain and do to make these tasks possible. These are the functional requirements:

  • the entities, attributes, primary keys and foreign keys needed in the database
  • the searches, sorts and calculations the system must carry out
  • these are usually written from the designer's point of view

The difference can feel subtle at first:

Requirement type Focus Example End-user requirement What the user needs to do A user needs to search for a record (one complete row of information in a table). Functional requirement What the database needs to store or do The database must include fields (single pieces of information in a table) that can be searched.

During the analysis stage of Database Development, identifying these requirements helps to:

  • clarify the design of the database
  • identify the features that need to be implemented
  • evaluate whether the system is fit for purpose after development is complete

docs.title: What to Look For
docs.text:
For a National 5 database question, end-user requirements are often found in comments from the people who will use the database.

Top Tip

For end-user requirements, look for phrases such as "I need to..." or "The user needs to be able to...".

Functional requirements describe how the database will support these tasks. This usually means identifying:

  • suitable entities or tables (the main things the database stores, such as books or pupils)
  • attributes or fields for each table (the details stored about each thing, such as title or surname)
  • primary keys (a unique ID for each record)
  • foreign keys used to link related tables (IDs that connect records in different tables)
  • searches, sorts or other actions the database must perform (what the database has to do with the stored data)

Some actions may look similar to the end-user requirements. The difference is the viewpoint:

  • an end-user requirement says what a person needs to do
  • a functional requirement says what the database must store, link, search, sort or calculate to make that task possible

By clearly identifying both types of requirement, you can make sure your database is designed with the right users, data and tasks in mind. This helps avoid problems later in development and makes it easier to check whether the finished database is fit for purpose.

Summary

During analysis, you identify who will use the database and what they need to do. End-user requirements describe the tasks users want to complete. Functional requirements describe the tables, fields, keys, relationships and actions the database must include to make those tasks possible.

docs.title: Task One
docs.text:
A used-car dealership has six showrooms in different locations across Scotland. It wants to create a relational database to store details of cars owned by the company and details of each of their showrooms.

The database will allow sales staff to view details of specific cars and to see which showroom the cars are located in.

The developers have asked some of the sales staff what they would like to see in the database.

Below are some of the comments they made.

  • I need to know details of all the cars we have of a particular make and model.
  • I need to be able to find all the cars we have in a particular price range.
  • I need to know the colour of a particular car.
  • I need to display car details in order of mileage, lowest first. If the mileage of the cars are the same, customers always want to know which car is the cheapest.
  • I need to display car details in order of price, cheapest first.
  • If I have a customer who wants a car in another showroom, I need to know the address and phone number of the other showrooms.
  • I need to know whether the car is diesel, petrol or electric.

Using analysis techniques, identify what is required in terms of end-user and functional requirements.

a) What are the end-user requirements?

b) What are the functional requirements? In your answer, identify 2 suitable entities, attributes, primary keys and any foreign keys required.

docs.title: Task Two
docs.text:
Highlands Library wants a new relational database to store details of books and authors. The developers of the database have asked the librarian to list some of the tasks that she would like the new database to be able to do.

Below are some of the comments they made.

  • I need to be able to search for the first name, surname and nationality of an author.
  • I need to be able to search for books based on the author's surname.
  • I need to be able to sort books by the number of pages that they have.
  • I need to be able to search for all the children's books and display the title, publisher and date of publication.
  • I need to be able to search for all the non-fiction books and sort them into alphabetical order of the author's surname.

Using analysis techniques, identify what is required in terms of end-user and functional requirements.

a) What are the end-user requirements?

b) What are the functional requirements? In your answer, identify 2 suitable entities, attributes, primary keys and any foreign keys required.

docs.title: Task Three
docs.text:
A local sports centre wants to create a relational database to store details of members and the fitness classes they book.

The database will allow reception staff to search for members, check which classes are running, and see which members have booked each class.

The developers have asked staff what they need the database to do.

Below are some of the comments they made.

  • I need to search for a member using their surname or membership number.
  • I need to see a member's phone number in case a class is cancelled.
  • I need to know which day and time each class takes place.
  • I need to see which instructor is taking a class.
  • I need to list all members booked into a particular class.
  • I need to find classes by activity type, such as yoga, spin or circuits.
  • I need to sort classes by date and start time.

Using analysis techniques, identify what is required in terms of end-user and functional requirements.

a) What are the end-user requirements?

b) What are the functional requirements? In your answer, identify 3 suitable entities, attributes, primary keys and any foreign keys required.

docs.title: Task Four
docs.text:
A veterinary surgery wants to create a relational database to store details of pets, their owners and appointments.

The database will allow reception staff and vets to look up pets, contact owners and view appointment details.

The developers have asked staff what they need the database to do.

Below are some of the comments they made.

  • I need to search for an owner by surname or phone number.
  • I need to see all pets registered to an owner.
  • I need to know a pet's name, species, breed and date of birth.
  • I need to view appointments in date and time order.
  • I need to see the reason for each appointment.
  • I need to know which vet the appointment is with.
  • I need to find all appointments for a particular pet.

Using analysis techniques, identify what is required in terms of end-user and functional requirements.

a) What are the end-user requirements?

b) What are the functional requirements? In your answer, identify 3 suitable entities, attributes, primary keys and any foreign keys required.

docs.title: Task Five
docs.text:
A school music department wants to create a relational database to organise instrument lessons for pupils.

The database will allow music teachers to store details of pupils, instruments and lesson times.

The developers have asked teachers what they need the database to do.

Below are some of the comments they made.

  • I need to search for a pupil by surname or class.
  • I need to see which instrument each pupil is learning.
  • I need to know the day, time and room for each lesson.
  • I need to list all pupils taught by a particular music teacher.
  • I need to find all lessons for a particular instrument.
  • I need to sort lessons by day and time.
  • I need to see a pupil's emergency contact number.

Using analysis techniques, identify what is required in terms of end-user and functional requirements.

a) What are the end-user requirements?

b) What are the functional requirements? In your answer, identify 3 suitable entities, attributes, primary keys and any foreign keys required.

docs.title: Analysis Worked Example
docs.text:
This page shows one way to work through an analysis question.

Teaching Note

In a real project, analysis is often an iterative process. Developers may move back and forward between user needs, functional requirements and database design. In this worked example, the steps are separated to make the process easier to follow.

docs.title: School Library Example
docs.text:
A secondary school wants to create a database to keep track of its library books, the pupils who borrow them, and the borrowing history.

The database will help school staff manage books and allow pupils to search for and borrow books.

docs.title: Step 1: Identify the Users
docs.text:
The users are the people who will use the database.

In this example, the users are:

  • Library staff
  • Pupils

docs.title: Step 2: Identify the End-User Requirements
docs.text:
These are the tasks the users need to complete.

The users need to be able to:

  • Search for books by title, author, or genre
  • Find out if a book is available or already on loan
  • View a list of books a pupil has borrowed
  • Check which books are overdue
  • Sort books by title or date added

Top Tip

End-user requirements are often written as "The user needs to be able to...".

docs.title: Step 3: Choose Tables and Fields
docs.text:
The database needs tables and fields to store the information.

For this example, the database needs three linked tables:

  1. Book – stores details of books in the library
  2. Pupil – stores details of pupils who borrow books
  3. Loan – stores details of which books have been borrowed and returned

Each table needs a primary key to uniquely identify each record. The Loan table will also need foreign keys to link each loan to the correct book and pupil.

The following tables and fields are required:

Book
  • Book ID (primary key)
  • Title
  • Author
  • Genre
  • Year Published
  • Available (Yes/No)
Pupil
  • Pupil ID (primary key)
  • First Name
  • Surname
  • Class
Loan
  • Loan ID (primary key)
  • Book ID (foreign key)
  • Pupil ID (foreign key)
  • Date Borrowed
  • Date Returned

docs.title: Step 4: Identify the Functional Requirements
docs.text:
These describe what the database must store, link and do.

The database must be able to:

  • Search books by title, author, or genre (uses the Title, Author and Genre fields)
  • Sort results alphabetically or by date (uses fields such as Title or Date Borrowed)
  • Check if a book is available (uses the Available field or the Loan table)
  • List all books borrowed by a pupil (uses links between the Pupil, Loan and Book tables)
  • Identify overdue books (uses the Date Borrowed and Date Returned fields)

docs.title: Step 5: Check the Link Between Requirements
docs.text:
Use one end-user requirement and check that the functional requirements support it.

Check the Connection

End-user requirement: Library staff need to identify overdue books.

Functional requirements: The Loan table stores Date Borrowed and Date Returned. The database can use these dates to find overdue loans.

Summary

Start with the users, then list what they need to do. After that, identify the tables, fields, keys and actions the database needs to support those tasks.

docs.title: Data Dictionaries
docs.text:
What You Need to Know

For National 5 Computing Science, you should be able to:

  • Describe what a data dictionary is.
  • Identify suitable field names.
  • Choose suitable data types.
  • Choose suitable field sizes.
  • Identify primary and foreign keys.
  • Choose suitable validation checks.
  • Justify the design choices made in a data dictionary.

docs.title: What Is A Data Dictionary?
docs.text:
A data dictionary is created during the design stage of database development.

It describes how each table in a database should be built.

You can think of it as a blueprint for the database.

It tells the developer:

  • what fields are needed
  • what type of data each field will store
  • how much data can be stored
  • which fields are keys
  • what validation should be used

Simple Example

Field Name Data Type Size Key Validation Student_ID Number 6 PK Presence check First_Name Text 20 Presence check Class_ID Number 3 FK Presence check

docs.title: Why Is A Data Dictionary Useful?
docs.text:
A data dictionary helps make sure the database is planned properly before it is created.

It helps to:

  • keep data consistent
  • reduce mistakes
  • make field names clear
  • choose sensible data types
  • show how tables are linked
  • help different developers understand the same design

Without a data dictionary, developers may store the same data in different ways.

For example, one developer might use PupilID, while another might use Student_Number. A data dictionary avoids this kind of confusion.

docs.title: Learn It In Smaller Steps
docs.text:
Work through these pages in order.

  1. Field Names And Data Types
  2. Field Sizes
  3. Primary And Foreign Keys
  4. Validation
  5. Worked Example

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
A data dictionary records the design of a database table.

It should include:

  • field names
  • data types
  • field sizes
  • primary and foreign keys
  • validation rules
  • clear reasons for each design choice

docs.title: Field Names
docs.text:
A field name should clearly describe the data being stored.

Good field names are short but meaningful.

Better Field Name Less Helpful Field Name Student_ID ID First_Name Name1 Date_Of_Birth Date House Info

Clear field names make the database easier to understand.

Remember

In database design, an attribute is a field or column in a table.

docs.title: Data Types
docs.text:
Each field must have a suitable data type.

Data Type Used For Example Text Words, names, addresses or codes that are not used in calculations Nevis Number Whole numbers or values used in calculations 15 Real Numbers with decimal places 12.50 Boolean True/False or Yes/No values True Date Dates 15/06/2026 Time Times 09:30

Common Mistake

A phone number or postcode is usually stored as text, not a number.

This is because you do not calculate with it, and it may contain spaces or start with 0.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Field names should clearly describe the data being stored.

Data types should match the kind of data in the field.

  • Use text for names, addresses and codes.
  • Use number for whole numbers used in calculations or comparisons.
  • Use real for decimal values.
  • Use Boolean for True/False or Yes/No values.
  • Use date and time for date or time values.

docs.title: What Is Field Size?
docs.text:
The field size sets the maximum amount of data that can be stored in a field.

Field size is most often used with text fields.

For example, a Forename field might have a size of 20. This means the field can store up to 20 characters.

docs.title: Choosing A Sensible Size
docs.text:
Field Data Type Sensible Size First_Name Text 20 Surname Text 30 Year_Group Number 2 Postcode Text 8

Choosing a suitable size matters.

  • If the size is too small, valid data may not fit.
  • If the size is much too large, storage space may be wasted.

Example

First_Name with size 3 would be too small because names like Eilidh or Daniel would not fit.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Field size controls the maximum amount of data that can be stored.

A good field size should be large enough for valid data, but not unnecessarily large.

  • Text fields often need a maximum number of characters.
  • A field size that is too small may cut off valid data.
  • A field size that is too large may waste storage space.

docs.title: Why Do Databases Use Keys?
docs.text:
Keys are used to identify records and link tables together.

Key Type Purpose Primary key Uniquely identifies each record in a table Foreign key Links to a primary key in another table

docs.title: Primary Key
docs.text:
A primary key must be unique.

This means no two records in the same table can have the same primary key value.

For example, Student_ID could be used as the primary key in a Pupils table because each pupil should have a different ID.

docs.title: Foreign Key
docs.text:
A foreign key is used to link one table to another table.

For example:

Pupils Table
------------
Student_ID (PK)
Forename
Surname
House_ID (FK)

        links to

Houses Table
------------
House_ID (PK)
House_Name

In this example:

  • Student_ID uniquely identifies each pupil.
  • House_ID in the Houses table uniquely identifies each house.
  • House_ID in the Pupils table links each pupil to a house.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Primary and foreign keys are used to identify records and link tables.

  • A primary key uniquely identifies each record in a table.
  • A foreign key links to a primary key in another table.
  • Keys help relational databases avoid duplication and keep linked data organised.

docs.title: What Is Validation?
docs.text:
Validation is used to reduce the chance of incorrect data being entered.

It does not check if the data is true.

It checks if the data is sensible or allowed.

docs.title: Common Validation Checks
docs.text:
Validation Type What It Checks Example Presence check Data has been entered Student_ID cannot be blank Range check Data is within an allowed range Age must be between 11 and 18 Length check Data has a suitable number of characters Postcode must be 6 to 8 characters Restricted choice Data must be chosen from a list House must be Nevis, Lochiel, Rannoch or Shiel

Common Mistake

Validation does not guarantee that data is correct.

For example, a range check can make sure an age is between 11 and 18, but it cannot prove the pupil entered their real age.

docs.title: Choosing Validation
docs.text:
Choose validation based on what the field should store.

Field Suitable Validation Forename Presence check Age Range check Postcode Length check House Restricted choice

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Validation reduces the chance of invalid data being entered.

  • A presence check makes sure data is entered.
  • A range check makes sure data is within allowed limits.
  • A length check makes sure data has a suitable number of characters.
  • A restricted choice makes sure data is chosen from a valid list.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
A school wants to store details about pupils.

The database needs to store:

  • a unique pupil ID
  • the pupil's forename
  • the pupil's surname
  • the pupil's year group
  • the pupil's house
  • whether the pupil receives free school meals

docs.title: Completed Data Dictionary
docs.text: Field Name Data Type Size Key Validation Student_ID Number 6 PK Presence check Forename Text 20 Presence check Surname Text 30 Presence check Year_Group Number 2 Range check: 1 to 6 House Text 10 Restricted choice Free_Meals Boolean Restricted choice

docs.title: Explaining The Choices
docs.text:
Student_ID is a primary key because it uniquely identifies each pupil.

Forename and Surname are text because they store names.

Year_Group is a number because it stores a year group value.

House is text because it stores a house name.

Free_Meals is Boolean because it stores either Yes or No.

docs.title: Exam Tip
docs.text:
When justifying a data dictionary, do not just name the data type.

Explain why it is suitable.

Weak Answer Better Answer Surname is text. Surname is text because it stores letters and will not be used in calculations. Age is number. Age is number because it stores a whole number and can be compared using range checks. Free_Meals is Boolean. Free_Meals is Boolean because it stores only two possible values: Yes or No.

docs.title: Database Relationships (One to Many)
docs.text:
What you need to know

Describe and exemplify entity-relationship diagrams with two entities indicating:

  • entity name
  • attribute name
  • primary and foreign key
  • attribute type:
    • text
    • number
    • date
    • time
    • boolean
  • attribute size
  • validation:
    • presence check
    • restricted choice
    • field length
    • range

Exemplify a design of a solution to the query:

  • multiple tables
  • fields
  • search criteria
  • sort order

Database relationships can be shown in the form of a diagram.

This diagram is known as an 'entity relationship diagram'.

As part of your exam, you will have to either draw or interpret one of these diagrams.

A one-to-many relationship is the most common type of relationship between database entities. In this type of relationship, a row in table A can have many matching rows in table B, but a row in table B can have only one matching row in table A.

For example, the publishers and titles tables have a one-to-many relationship: each publisher produces many titles, but each title comes from only one publisher.

Think about a mother and her children. A mother can have many children. A child can have only one mother.

Dog Walkers is a company that walks dogs when their owners are at work. The company has a database to store details of the dogs, their owners and the walkers. The data is stored in the following tables.

The one-to-many relationships that exist between the tables are:

  • Walker and Walk - there is only one walker to many walks. For example, a dog walker can do many different walks.

  • Dog and Walk - there is one dog to many walks. For example, one dog can go on many different walks.

  • Owner and Dog - there is one owner to many dogs. For example, one owner may have many different dogs.

Mother and Child Full Example

docs.title: Referential integrity
docs.text:
Referential integrity is a rule in relational databases that ensures relationships between tables remain valid and consistent. It prevents orphan records, meaning every foreign key in a table must match a valid primary key in another table.

For example, in a school database:

  • The Students table has a primary key (StudentID).

  • Enrolments table records which courses students take and includes a foreign key (StudentID).

Referential integrity ensures that every StudentID in Enrolments exists in Students, preventing enrolments for non-existent students.

docs.title: Why is this important?
docs.text:
• Prevents data inconsistencies and errors.
  • Ensures linked data remains accurate and complete.
  • Maintains the reliability of relationships between tables.

docs.title: Understanding the Problem
docs.text:
Before designing a query, you must first make sure you understand the question being asked. For example:

  • What information do you need? (e.g., student names and grades)
  • Which table(s) contain the required data?
  • Do you need to filter, sort, or combine data?

docs.title: Identifying the Required SQL Commands
docs.text: To design a query effectively, you must know the key SQL commands:

docs.title: SELECT ... FROM ...
docs.text: The SELECT statement specifies which columns to display, while FROM indicates the table where the data is stored.

docs.title: WHERE (Filtering Data)
docs.text: Use the WHERE clause to filter results based on a condition.

docs.title: ORDER BY (Sorting Data)
docs.text: To arrange the results in ascending (ASC) or descending (DESC) order, use ORDER BY.

docs.title: UPDATE - Modifying Data
docs.text: Use UPDATE to change existing records.

docs.title: DELETE - Removing Data
docs.text:
Use DELETE to remove specific records.

Example Question

QuestionAnswer

Design a query that would output the model, number of seats and the showroom manager for all the Jaguar cars located in Glasgow.

docs.title: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)
docs.text:
What you need to know

Describe and identify the implications for individuals and businesses of the UK General Data Protection Regulation (UK GDPR) that data must be:

  • processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to individuals.

  • used for the declared purpose only

  • limited to the data needed for the declared purpose

  • accurate

  • not kept for longer than necessary

  • held securely

The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a law that covers how your data is stored and used.

In some older notes, you may see it referred to as the Data Protection Act - this name changed in 2018, and you should use GDPR instead.

The data subject is the person whose data is being stored - for example, in the school register database, pupils are the data subjects, as the data is about them. The person in control of the data is called the data controller.

There is a declared purpose - what the data is being used for.

For example, in a school, the declared purpose might be that we store the register data to check attendance.

That is the declared purpose of our database.

You need to know six implications (consequences) of GDPR:

  • Data must be processed lawfully, fairly and transparently (this means being open about it)

  • Data must be used for the declared purpose only

  • Only the data needed for the declared purpose should be collected.

  • Data must be accurate

  • Data must not be kept for longer than necessary

  • Data must be held securely

Example

For the 2019 youth club question, you would have to identify two implications and link them to the youth club.

Note - they would have to be implications for the youth club, not the members.

For example:

  • They must only collect data needed for running the club

  • They must only use the data for the declared purpose (running the club)

  • Data must be deleted if members leave the club

  • Data cannot be shared without the members’ permission

You would receive one mark for each implication.

docs.title: SQL SELECT Operations
docs.text:
What You Need to Know

For National 5 Computing Science, you should be able to:

  • Retrieve data from a database using SQL.
  • Use SELECT ... FROM ... queries.
  • Use SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ... queries.
  • Apply conditional operators to filter records.
  • Use logical operators (AND and OR) to combine conditions.
  • Sort query results using ORDER BY.

docs.title: SELECT ... FROM ...
docs.text:
The SELECT ... FROM ... statement is used to retrieve data from a table.

You can either:

  • Display all columns in a table.
  • Display only specific columns.

docs.title: Display All Columns
Display Specific Columns
SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ...
docs.text:
The WHERE clause is used to filter records.

Only records that meet the specified condition will be displayed.

Select With a Condition
SELECT column1, column2, column3
FROM entity_name
WHERE column3 > value;

Example

Pupils Aged Over 14
SELECT Forename, Surname
FROM Pupils
WHERE Age > 14;

This query would return only pupils older than 14.

docs.title: Conditional Operators
docs.text:
Conditional operators are used inside a WHERE clause to compare values.

Operator Meaning = Equal to > Greater than < Less than >= Greater than or equal to <= Less than or equal to <> Not equal to

Example

Find Pupils Aged 15
SELECT *
FROM Pupils
WHERE Age = 15;

docs.title: Logical Operators
docs.text: Logical operators allow multiple conditions to be combined.

docs.title: AND
docs.text:
AND requires all conditions to be true.

AND Example
SELECT *
FROM Pupils
WHERE Age > 14
AND House = 'Nevis';

This query returns pupils who are older than 14 and belong to Nevis house.

docs.title: OR
docs.text:
OR requires at least one condition to be true.

OR Example
SELECT *
FROM Pupils
WHERE House = 'Nevis'
OR House = 'Locheil';

This query returns pupils from either Nevis or Locheil.

docs.title: ORDER BY
docs.text: The ORDER BY clause is used to sort query results.

docs.title: Ascending Order (ASC)
docs.text:
Ascending order is the default order.

Ascending Order
SELECT Forename, Surname, Age
FROM Pupils
ORDER BY Age ASC;

This displays the youngest pupils first.

docs.title: Descending Order (DESC)
docs.text:
Descending Order
SELECT Forename, Surname, Age
FROM Pupils
ORDER BY Age DESC;

This displays the oldest pupils first.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
SQL Feature Purpose SELECT ... FROM ... Retrieves data from a table WHERE Filters records Conditional Operators Compare values AND All conditions must be true OR At least one condition must be true ORDER BY Sorts query results

These commands form the foundation of SQL queries at National 5 level.

docs.title: SQL INSERT Operations
docs.text:
What you need to Know

For National 5 Computing Science, you should be able to:

  • Use SQL commands to add records to a table.
  • Understand the purpose of the INSERT statement.
  • Apply these commands to simple database tables.

docs.title: What is INSERT?
docs.text:
The INSERT statement is used to add a new record to a table.

When inserting data, you specify:

  1. The table you want to add the record to.
  2. The columns that will receive data.
  3. The values to be stored.
Insert Syntax
INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2, column3)
VALUES (value1, value2, value3);

docs.title: Example
Summary
docs.text:
Part Purpose INSERT INTO Chooses the table to add data to Column list Chooses which fields will receive data VALUES Provides the data for the new record

The INSERT statement is used when a new record needs to be added to a database table.

docs.title: SQL UPDATE Operations
docs.text:
What you need to Know

For National 5 Computing Science, you should be able to:

  • Use SQL commands to update existing records.
  • Understand the purpose of the UPDATE statement.
  • Use a WHERE clause to identify which records should be changed.
  • Apply update commands to simple database tables.

docs.title: What is UPDATE?
docs.text:
The UPDATE statement is used to change information that already exists in a table.

When updating data, you specify:

  1. The table that contains the record.
  2. The field that needs to change.
  3. The new value.
  4. The condition that identifies which record should be updated.
Update Syntax
UPDATE table_name
SET column_name = new_value
WHERE condition;

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Update a Pupil Age
UPDATE Pupils
SET Age = 16
WHERE StudentNum = 13;

This would change Emma Brown's age to 16.

Important

Always use a WHERE clause when updating records. Without a WHERE clause, every record in the table will be updated.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Part Purpose UPDATE Chooses the table to change SET Chooses the field and new value WHERE Chooses which record or records should be changed

The UPDATE statement is used when existing data needs to be changed.

docs.title: SQL DELETE Operations
docs.text:
What you need to Know

For National 5 Computing Science, you should be able to:

  • Use SQL commands to delete existing records.
  • Understand the purpose of the DELETE statement.
  • Use a WHERE clause to identify which records should be removed.
  • Apply delete commands to simple database tables.

docs.title: What is DELETE?
docs.text:
The DELETE statement is used to remove records from a table.

When deleting data, you specify:

  1. The table that contains the record.
  2. The condition that identifies which record should be deleted.
Delete Syntax
DELETE FROM table_name
WHERE condition;

docs.title: Example
Summary
docs.text:
Part Purpose DELETE FROM Chooses the table to remove data from WHERE Chooses which record or records should be removed

The DELETE statement is used when existing data needs to be removed from a database table.

docs.title: SQL Equi-Join Operations
docs.text:
What You Need to Know

For National 5 Computing Science, you should be able to:

  • Use an equi-join to retrieve data from two related tables.
  • Identify the matching fields used to join two tables.
  • Understand that an equi-join uses a WHERE clause.
  • Explain how records are matched using equal values in both tables.
  • Recognise primary key and foreign key fields in a join.

docs.title: What Is An Equi-Join?
docs.text:
An equi-join is used to display data from two related tables in one query.

The tables are joined using fields that contain matching values.

For example, a Pupils table might store pupil details, and a Guidance table might store guidance teacher details.

If both tables contain a House field, SQL can use that field to match the correct pupils with the correct guidance teacher.

At National 5, an equi-join is written using:

  • SELECT
  • FROM
  • WHERE

The WHERE clause is used to show which fields must match.

docs.title: Before The SQL
docs.text:
Before writing an equi-join, it helps to remember the database words.

Word Meaning Entity A table in the database Attribute A field or column in a table Record One row of data in a table Primary key A field that uniquely identifies a record Foreign key A field that links to a primary key in another table

Example

In a Pupils table:

  • the entity is Pupils
  • Forename, Surname and House are attributes
  • one pupil's details make one record

When joining tables, look for an attribute that appears in both tables or links the two tables together.

This is the matching field.

docs.title: Equi-Join Syntax
docs.text:
Equi-Join Syntax
SELECT *
FROM entity1, entity2
WHERE entity1.column1 = entity2.column1;

In this syntax:

  • entity1 is the first table.
  • entity2 is the second table.
  • column1 is the matching field.
  • The field on the left of the equals sign comes from one table.
  • The field on the right of the equals sign comes from the other table.

Only records with matching values will be displayed.

docs.title: Example Tables
docs.text:
The Guidance table stores information about guidance teachers.

Entity = Guidance

EmployeeNum Title Surname House 1 Mr Stewart Nevis 2 Miss Kelly Lochiel 3 Mr Macdonald Rannoch 4 Mrs Jordan Shiel

The House field is an attribute in the Guidance table.

The Pupils table also contains a House attribute.

Entity = Pupils

StudentNum Forename Surname House 1 Amy Smith Nevis 2 Ben Campbell Lochiel 3 Cara Fraser Rannoch 4 Daniel Brown Nevis

The two tables can be linked because both entities store house names.

  • One guidance teacher is responsible for many pupils.
  • Each pupil belongs to one house.
  • The House attribute contains matching values in both tables.

How To Spot The Matching Field

Ask yourself:

  • Which attribute appears in both tables?
  • Which values are repeated in both tables?
  • Which field links the records together?

docs.title: Equi-Join Example
docs.text:
The following query displays pupil and guidance information together.

Join Pupils And Guidance
SELECT *
FROM Pupils, Guidance
WHERE Pupils.House = Guidance.House;

This query selects all fields from both tables.

The most important line is the condition:

Pupils.House = Guidance.House

matches records where the pupil's house is the same as the guidance teacher's house.

For example, a pupil in Nevis would be matched with Mr Stewart because both records contain the value Nevis in the House field.

docs.title: Reading The Query Step By Step
docs.text:
SQL Line What It Does SELECT * Display all attributes from the joined records FROM Pupils, Guidance Use the Pupils and Guidance entities WHERE Pupils.House = Guidance.House Match records where the House values are equal

Key Idea

The equals sign in an equi-join does not mean "store this value".

It means "only show records where these two values match".

docs.title: Selecting Specific Fields
docs.text:
You do not always need to display every field.

You can choose only the fields needed in the output.

Select Specific Fields
SELECT Pupils.Forename, Pupils.Surname, Guidance.Title, Guidance.Surname
FROM Pupils, Guidance
WHERE Pupils.House = Guidance.House;

This would display:

  • the pupil's forename
  • the pupil's surname
  • the guidance teacher's title
  • the guidance teacher's surname

Using the table name before the attribute name makes it clear which table the attribute comes from.

Exam Tip

When identifying an equi-join, look for a WHERE condition that compares a field from one table with a matching field from another table.

WHERE Pupils.House = Guidance.House;

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
SQL Feature Purpose SELECT Chooses the fields to display FROM Names the tables being used WHERE Gives the matching condition = Matches equal values in both tables Entity A table used in the query Attribute A field used in the query Equi-join Combines records from related tables

Equi-joins are used when information is stored across more than one related table.

docs.text:
Open the SQL workspace

This query joins the Pupils and Guidance tables using the matching House field.

b) Display selected fields from both tables

This query displays each pupil's name and their guidance teacher's surname.

SELECT Pupils.Forename, Pupils.Surname, Guidance.Surname

This query uses aliases to make the output easier to read.

SELECT Pupils.Forename AS PupilForename,
       Pupils.Surname AS PupilSurname,
       Guidance.Title AS TeacherTitle,
       Guidance.Surname AS TeacherSurname
FROM Pupils, Guidance
WHERE Pupils.House = Guidance.House;

docs.title: Select, From and Where Tasks
docs.text:
Bishra's Bakery keeps delivery information in a database. Bishra will ask for different reports, and your job is to turn each request into an SQL query.

These tasks practise using:

  • SELECT to choose the fields to display
  • FROM to choose the table
  • WHERE to search for specific records
  • AND and OR to join conditions together

The two tables you will use are:

  • DeliveryDrivers
  • DeliverySchools

For each task, write and run the SQL query, then screenshot your SQL code and the results table for your workbook.

Open the SQL workspace

docs.title: Task One
docs.text:
"We need all the information about the drivers displayed."

Bishra wants a full list of every delivery driver.

Your query should display every field from the DeliveryDrivers table.

docs.title: Task Two
docs.text:
"Can we have all the deliveries but just the school names, towns and addresses set out?"

Bishra wants a shorter delivery list that only shows the location details.

Your query should use the DeliverySchools table and display:

  • school name
  • town
  • address

docs.title: Task Three
docs.text:
"Can we have all the information on the Hilton Primary delivery please?"

Bishra wants to look up one specific delivery.

Your query should use the DeliverySchools table, display all fields, and only show the record for Hilton Primary.

docs.title: Task Four
docs.text:
"We need the names, ages and IDs of all the drivers who are 30 years old or younger."

Bishra wants to find the younger drivers in the bakery.

Your query should use the DeliveryDrivers table and display:

  • driver name
  • age
  • employee ID

Only show drivers whose age is 30 or younger.

docs.title: Task Five
docs.text:
"We might be adding extra Inverness schools if we can fit in the deliveries. We need to know all the Inverness deliveries at ten in the morning or after to see what other deliveries we can do. Display the school names, the town and time of delivery of these schools found."

Bishra is checking whether more Inverness deliveries could fit into the morning route.

Your query should use the DeliverySchools table and display:

  • school name
  • town
  • delivery time

Only show deliveries where the town is Inverness and the delivery time is 10:00 or later.

docs.title: Task Six
docs.text:
"New laws state all drivers under the age of 25 or 35 or over need to be re-tested in their vans for their driving licence each year. We need to find all the drivers affected along with their van registration number to send to the testing centre."

Bishra needs a list of drivers who may need extra licence checks.

Your query should use the DeliveryDrivers table and display:

  • driver name
  • van registration number

Only show drivers who are under 25 or drivers who are 35 and older.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
Testing ensures that a database and its queries work correctly and return accurate results. Without testing, errors could lead to incorrect data retrieval, processing, or even data loss.

Testing helps to:

  • Identify and fix errors in SQL queries.
  • Ensure queries return the expected results.
  • Verify that the database structure meets requirements.
  • Improve database performance and efficiency.

docs.title: Types of Testing
docs.text:
1. Syntax Testing - Checks that SQL commands follow the correct rules and structure. If there are mistakes in SQL keywords, punctuation, or spelling, an error message will appear.

  2. Execution Testing (Functional Testing) - Ensures that queries return the expected results. Uses sample data to check if the query outputs correct and complete results.

  3. Normal, Extreme, and Exceptional Testing. To fully test a query, different types of data must be used:

    1. Normal Data (Valid Data) – Typical inputs that should work.

    2. Extreme Data (Edge Cases) – Values at the limits of what the database should handle.

    3. Exceptional Data (Invalid Data) – Inputs that should cause an error or be rejected.

docs.title: Evaluation
docs.text:
Once testing is complete, the database must be evaluated to ensure it meets all requirements.

Evaluation involves checking the following key aspects:

  1. Does the Database Meet User Requirements?

    • Does it store all necessary data?
    • Do queries return the correct results?
    • Are relationships between tables correctly defined?

  2. Is the Database Efficient?

    • Do queries execute quickly?
    • Are only the necessary columns selected? (e.g., avoiding SELECT * when specific fields are needed)
    • Are indexes used effectively to speed up searches?

  3. Is the Database Secure?

    • Are access rights correctly configured?
    • Can unauthorized users modify or access data?

  4. Is the Data Accurate and Reliable?

    • Does the data reflect real-world values?
    • Are there any errors in the query results?

If the system does not meet these expectations, it may be difficult to use and require modifications.

docs.title: Applying Evaluation in Your Coursework
docs.text:
In the National 5 Computing Science exam, you may be given database tables along with SQL queries. You will need to:

  • Predict the expected outcome of the queries.
  • Identify any errors or issues in the SQL statements.
  • Explain what is wrong and suggest how to fix it.
  • By understanding how to test and evaluate a database system, you will be well-prepared to analyze database queries and improve system functionality.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
You should practise:

  • SELECT ... FROM
  • WHERE
  • comparison operators such as =, <, >, <= and >=
  • AND and OR
  • ORDER BY
  • INSERT
  • UPDATE
  • DELETE
  • simple equi-joins using primary and foreign keys

Question 1 Display the video name and video duration of the video called Microbits.

Question 2 Remove the vlogger, Jenna Matthews, ID 13 from the database.

To test your answer, run:

SELECT *
FROM Vlogger
WHERE vloggerID = 13;

If your query has been successful, Jenna Matthews should no longer appear. The test query should return no records.

Question 3 Display the full names of all the Makeup experts.

Question 4 Display all the videos, name and date created, with a rating of 1. Display from oldest to newest.

Question 5 Add this new vlogger: vloggerID 16, Jenna Atkins, jening, Programming.

To test your answer, run:

SELECT *
FROM Vlogger
WHERE vloggerID = 16;

If your query has been successful, the test query should show one record for Jenna Atkins with vloggerID 16, username jening and expertise Programming.

Question 6 Display the forename and surname of Gaming vloggers who created a video less than 60 minutes long, ordered by surname.

Question 7 Vlogger 12 created a new video called Jumpers. Add the new video to the database.

To test your answer, run:

SELECT *
FROM Video
WHERE videoName = 'Jumpers';

If your query has been successful, the test query should show one video called Jumpers for vloggerID 12, with duration 43 and rating 4.

Question 8 Remove all vloggers who specialise in Programming videos from the database.

To test your answer, run:

SELECT *
FROM Vlogger
WHERE expertise = 'Programming';

If your query has been successful, there should be no vloggers left with Programming as their expertise. The test query should return no records.

Question 9 Change the expertise from Gaming to Games.

To test your answer, run:

SELECT *
FROM Vlogger
WHERE expertise = 'Games';

If your query has been successful, the test query should show the vloggers who now have Games as their expertise instead of Gaming.

Question 10 Display all video IDs and names of every video created on 25 April 2019 by baking experts.

Question 11 Add a new vlogger, Giles Cannon, with a made up ID and username. Expertise is not known yet.

To test your answer, run:

SELECT *
FROM Vlogger
WHERE forename = 'Giles'
AND surname = 'Cannon';

If your query has been successful, the test query should show one record for Giles Cannon with the ID and username you chose.

Question 12 Change any videos currently rated 5 to a rating of 4.

To test your answer, run:

SELECT *
FROM Video
WHERE rating = 5;

If your query has been successful, there should be no videos left with a rating of 5. The test query should return no records.

Question 13 Display the vlogger name, expertise, video name and duration of videos between 30 and 180 minutes long, longest first.

Question 14 Display the complete details of any vlogger who has had at least a rating of 4.

Question 15 Noah Sim has requested that all his videos are removed from the database. Implement Noah's request.

To test your answer, run:

SELECT *
FROM Video
WHERE vloggerID IN (
  SELECT vloggerID
  FROM Vlogger
  WHERE forename = 'Noah'
  AND surname = 'Sim'
);

If your query has been successful, Noah Sim should have no videos left in the Video table. The test query should return no records.

Question 16 Display the names of all videos created by a programming expert, with rating 4, less than 60 minutes long, ordered alphabetically.

Credit: Greig Reid

docs.title: After School Clubs
docs.text:
Use this page to practise the SQL skills needed for National 5 Computing Science.

The tasks are based on a small database about school clubs. Some questions ask you to display data, while others ask you to add, update or delete records.

For questions that change the database, a short test query is included. Run the test query after your answer to check whether your SQL has worked.

Open the SQL workspace

docs.title: The Database
docs.text:
The database has two tables:

Club

docs.text:
You should practise:

  • SELECT ... FROM
  • WHERE
  • comparison operators such as =, <, >, <= and >=
  • AND and OR
  • ORDER BY
  • INSERT
  • UPDATE
  • DELETE
  • simple equi-joins using primary and foreign keys

Question 1 Display every field from the Member table.

Question 2 Display only the forename, surname and year group of each member.

Question 3 Display every field for clubs in the Computing category.

Question 4 Display the forename and surname of members in S3.

Question 5 Display the club name and room for clubs in the Computing category that are held on Friday.

Question 6 Display the forename, surname and year group of members who are in S3 or S5.

Question 7 Display the members' forenames, surnames and year groups from oldest year group to youngest year group.

Question 8 Add a new club with these details:

clubID clubName category day room 8 Netball Club Sport Wednesday Gym

To test your answer, run:

SELECT *
FROM Club
WHERE clubID = 8;

If your query has been successful, the test query should show Netball Club.

Question 9 Change Football Club so that it is held in the Gym instead of the Field.

To test your answer, run:

SELECT clubName, room
FROM Club
WHERE clubName = 'Football Club';

If your query has been successful, the room should be Gym.

Question 10 Remove the member called Harris Young from the database.

To test your answer, run:

SELECT *
FROM Member
WHERE forename = 'Harris'
AND surname = 'Young';

If your query has been successful, the test query should return no records.

Question 11 Display each member's forename, surname, club name and club day.

Question 12 Display the forename and surname of every member who attends a Sport club. Sort the results by surname in alphabetical order.
